description: Base English translation style — universal rules for all genres. Applied first; genre guides layer on top.

fundamental_principles.voice: Preserve the author's distinct voice and rhythm — do not homogenize
fundamental_principles.naturalness: Write as a fluent native English speaker; never carry JP syntax into EN
fundamental_principles.consistency: Use established character names and terms throughout the volume
fundamental_principles.register: Match the emotional and social register of the source text
fundamental_principles.no_translationese: Avoid word-for-word literal translation; find the English idiom

core_grammar_rules.no_jp_sentence_structure: JP subject-object-verb order must be rearranged naturally
core_grammar_rules.pronoun_usage: Use gendered or gender-neutral pronouns consistently per character
core_grammar_rules.tense_consistency: Narrative past tense throughout unless genre demands otherwise
core_grammar_rules.no_over_formality: Avoid stilted formal English in casual dialogue

cultural_terms.first_mention: Introduce JP cultural terms with brief English context on first use
cultural_terms.subsequent_mentions: Use the established EN equivalent or romanized term consistently
cultural_terms.honorifics: Retain Japanese honorifics (san, kun, chan, senpai) in dialogue when they carry relational meaning not expressible in EN

quality_checklist.no_translationese: No literal JP syntax carried into EN
quality_checklist.no_jp_residue: No romanized JP terms without prior introduction
quality_checklist.character_voice: Each character has a distinct, consistent speech pattern
quality_checklist.cultural_terms: First-use cultural terms carry English context gloss
quality_checklist.no_filter_words: Minimal use of filter words (saw, felt, thought that) in close third/first person
quality_checklist.rhythm: Sentence rhythm matches the pacing of the source scene
